---
title: "Exploratory Data Analysis of DHS Estimates"
description: "Initial data cleaning, extraction, and analysis of Department of Homeland Security reports."
author: "Mario Arce Acosta"
date: "2026-04-12"
categories: [R, EDA, Data Cleaning]
#image: "dhs_thumbnail.jpg" # Save an image in the same folder to show on the blog grid
---

## The Objective

Before deploying any advanced modeling, it is critical to understand the shape, missingness, and distributions of the raw Department of Homeland Security data.

## Loading the Data

We'll start by pulling in t

```{r}
#| label: data-prep
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(tidyverse)
library(DBI)
library(RPostgres)

# 1. Connect and fetch your highly granular ACS summary
con <- dbConnect(
  RPostgres::Postgres(),
  dbname = "postgres",
  host = "aws-1-us-east-2.pooler.supabase.com", 
  port = 6543,
  user = "postgres.dpuhypyqwupzesfkiplu",
  password = Sys.getenv("SUPABASE_PWD")
)
acs_raw_summary <- dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT * FROM acs_imputations_summary")
dbDisconnect(con)

# 2. Load the DHS Official CSVs
totals <- read_csv("dhs_historical_totals.csv") %>%
  filter(Data_Year >=2009 & Data_Year <= 2019) 
dhs_states <- read_csv("dhs_historical_state.csv") %>%
  filter(Data_Year >=2009 & Data_Year <= 2019) 
dhs_countries <- read_csv("dhs_historical_country.csv") %>%
  filter(Data_Year >=2009 & Data_Year <= 2019) 

# 3. Build a Crosswalk for States
fips_map <- c(
  "1"="Alabama", "2"="Alaska", "4"="Arizona", "5"="Arkansas", "6"="California", 
  "8"="Colorado", "9"="Connecticut", "10"="Delaware", "11"="District of Columbia", 
  "12"="Florida", "13"="Georgia", "15"="Hawaii", "16"="Idaho", "17"="Illinois", 
  "18"="Indiana", "19"="Iowa", "20"="Kansas", "21"="Kentucky", "22"="Louisiana", 
  "23"="Maine", "24"="Maryland", "25"="Massachusetts", "26"="Michigan", 
  "27"="Minnesota", "28"="Mississippi", "29"="Missouri", "30"="Montana", 
  "31"="Nebraska", "32"="Nevada", "33"="New Hampshire", "34"="New Jersey", 
  "35"="New Mexico", "36"="New York", "37"="North Carolina", "38"="North Dakota", 
  "39"="Ohio", "40"="Oklahoma", "41"="Oregon", "42"="Pennsylvania", 
  "44"="Rhode Island", "45"="South Carolina", "46"="South Dakota", "47"="Tennessee", 
  "48"="Texas", "49"="Utah", "50"="Vermont", "51"="Virginia", "53"="Washington", 
  "54"="West Virginia", "55"="Wisconsin", "56"="Wyoming"
)
names(totals)[names(totals) == "Data_Year"] <- "year"
names(dhs_states)[names(dhs_states) == "Data_Year"] <- "year"
names(dhs_countries)[names(dhs_countries) == "Data_Year"] <- "year"
names(dhs_states)[names(dhs_states) == "Category_Value"] <- "state_name"
names(dhs_countries)[names(dhs_countries) == "Category_Value"] <- "country_name"


# Translate the ACS state codes into text so it matches the DHS CSV
acs_base <- acs_raw_summary %>%
  mutate(state_name = fips_map[as.character(statefip)])
```

```{r}
# Roll up ACS data to the National level
macro_acs <- acs_base %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(
    total_foreign_born = sum(total_foreign_born_pop, na.rm = TRUE),
    # Let's use your high probability model for this example
    imputed_count = sum(high_prob_count, na.rm = TRUE) 
  )

# Join and Calculate Rates
macro_comparison <- macro_acs %>%
  # Assuming dhs_totals has columns: year, dhs_unauth_count
  left_join(totals, by = "year") %>%
  mutate(
    imputation_rate = imputed_count / total_foreign_born,
    dhs_rate = Count / total_foreign_born,
    variance = imputation_rate - dhs_rate
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: dhs-barplots
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(tidyverse)
library(DBI)
library(RPostgres)
library(plotly)

# --- 1. FETCH THE DATA ---
con <- dbConnect(
  RPostgres::Postgres(),
  dbname = "postgres",
  host = "aws-1-us-east-2.pooler.supabase.com", 
  port = 6543,
  user = "postgres.dpuhypyqwupzesfkiplu",
  password = Sys.getenv("SUPABASE_PWD")
)

acs_summary <- dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT * FROM acs_imputations_summary")
dbDisconnect(con)

# --- 2. PLOT 1: THE OVERALL TOTAL ---
# Roll up the data ignoring all demographic categories
total_yearly <- acs_summary %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(total_high_recall = sum(high_recall_count, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = 'drop')

# Build the plot using ggplot2
p_total <- ggplot(total_yearly, aes(x = as.character(year), y = total_high_recall)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2E86C1") +
  labs(
    title = "Total Imputed DHS Records by Year",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Total Imputations"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# Convert to an interactive web chart
ggplotly(p_total, tooltip = c("y", "x"))


# --- 3. PLOT 2: THE DEMOGRAPHIC BREAKDOWN ---
age_yearly <- acs_summary %>%
  group_by(year, age_bracket) %>%
  summarise(total_high_recall = sum(high_recall_count, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = 'drop')

# Build a grouped bar chart
p_age <- ggplot(age_yearly, aes(x = as.character(year), y = total_high_recall, fill = age_bracket)) +
  # position = "dodge" puts the bars side-by-side instead of stacking them
  geom_col(position = "dodge", alpha = 0.9) + 
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "mako") + # Adds a professional color palette
  labs(
    title = "Imputed Records by Year and Age Bracket",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Total Imputations",
    fill = "Age Bracket"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# Convert to an interactive web chart
ggplotly(p_age, tooltip = c("fill", "y", "x"))
```

```{r}
library(tidyverse)

# --- PRE-REQUISITE: ASSUMED STARTING DATA ---
# acs_base: Your ACS data from the previous step (has year, state_name, bpld, high_prob_count)
# dhs_states: CSV with year, state_name, dhs_count
# dhs_countries: CSV with year, country_name, dhs_count

# --- 1. COUNTRY OF ORIGIN HARMONIZATION ---
# Create a crosswalk for the Top DHS Countries using BPLD codes
country_map <- c(
  "20000" = "Mexico",
  "21010" = "El Salvador",
  "21020" = "Guatemala",
  "21030" = "Honduras",
  "50100" = "India",
  "50000" = "China",
  "51500" = "Philippines"
  # Add the rest of the DHS explicitly named countries here...
)

acs_mapped <- acs_base %>%
  mutate(
    # Translate the code to a name. If it's not in our map, label it "Other"
    country_name = coalesce(country_map[as.character(bpld)], "Other Countries")
  )

# --- 2. STATE DISTRIBUTION COMPARISON ---
# Calculate the percentage of the total population living in each state
state_distribution <- acs_mapped %>%
  # 1. Roll up ACS counts by State
  group_by(year, state_name) %>%
  summarise(imputed_count = sum(high_recall_count, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  # 2. Calculate the ACS State Percentage
  group_by(year) %>%
  mutate(imputed_pct = (imputed_count / sum(imputed_count)) * 100) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  
  # 3. Join with DHS States
  left_join(dhs_states, by = c("year", "state_name")) %>%
  
  # 4. Calculate the DHS State Percentage & The Variance
  group_by(year) %>%
  mutate(
    dhs_pct = (Count / sum(Count, na.rm = TRUE)) * 100,
    # Positive variance means your model over-allocated to this state vs DHS
    dist_variance = imputed_pct - dhs_pct 
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(!is.na(state_name))

# --- 3. COUNTRY DISTRIBUTION COMPARISON ---
# Calculate the percentage of the total population originating from each country
country_distribution <- acs_mapped %>%
  # 1. Roll up ACS counts by Country (including the "Other" bucket)
  group_by(year, country_name) %>%
  summarise(imputed_count = sum(high_prob_count, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  # 2. Calculate the ACS Country Percentage
  group_by(year) %>%
  mutate(imputed_pct = (imputed_count / sum(imputed_count)) * 100) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  
  # 3. Join with DHS Countries
  left_join(dhs_countries, by = c("year", "country_name")) %>%
  
  # 4. Calculate the DHS Country Percentage & The Variance
  group_by(year) %>%
  mutate(
    dhs_pct = (Count / sum(Count, na.rm = TRUE)) * 100,
    dist_variance = imputed_pct - dhs_pct
  ) %>%
  ungroup()
```

```{r}
# Example: Visualizing the State Variance for 2019
state_variance_plot <- state_distribution %>%
  filter(year == 2019) %>%
  # Filter to the top 15 states by DHS volume so the chart isn't cluttered
  slice_max(order_by = Count, n = 5) %>%
  mutate(
    # Create a flag for coloring: Over-estimated vs Under-estimated
    variance_direction = ifelse(dist_variance > 0, "Over-Allocated", "Under-Allocated")
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(state_name, dist_variance), y = dist_variance, fill = variance_direction)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() + # Makes it a horizontal bar chart
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Over-Allocated" = "#2ca02c", "Under-Allocated" = "#d62728")) +
  labs(
    title = "Imputation Variance by State (2019)",
    subtitle = "Percentage point difference from official DHS state distributions",
    x = "",
    y = "Variance (Percentage Points)",
    fill = "Model Behavior vs DHS"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(state_variance_plot)
```
